Every family that can afford to slaughters a sheep (al-kbush) in commemoration of Ibrahim's sacrifice. The meat is divided: a third for the family, a third for friends, a third for the poor. For days before the Eid, every street in Morocco fills with live sheep — bought and kept in homes and on terraces until the day of sacrifice. The nationwide scale of the sheep purchase and slaughter is one of Morocco's most remarkable social phenomena. Traditional Eid el-Kebir foods: mrouzia tagine (lamb with raisins, almonds, and honey — a Eid specialty), kebda (liver — slaughtered immediately and grilled fresh — the first meat eaten), various offal preparations, and lamb cooked in every way imaginable.

festivals.name: Asilah Arts Festival (Moussem Culturel International d'Asilah)
festivals.description: One of Africa's finest arts festivals — held in the whitewashed Atlantic coastal town of Asilah, one of Morocco's most beautiful medinas. The festival transforms Asilah's medina walls into a canvas — international and Moroccan muralists paint large-scale murals on the historic whitewashed walls, creating an open-air gallery that changes every year. Alongside the murals, the festival features poetry readings, music performances, cultural forums and political-intellectual symposia attracting Arab and African thinkers, and art exhibitions. Founded in 1978 by Asilah's mayor Mohammed Benaissa.

festivals.name: Rose Festival (Festival des Roses)
festivals.location: El-Kelaa M'Gouna and Kalaat M'Gouna, Dadès Valley
festivals.description: The Dadès Valley between Ouarzazate and Boumalne is Morocco's rose-growing heartland — the Rosa damascena (Damask rose) is cultivated here at high altitude (1,500m) and harvested in May. The rose festival celebrates the harvest with a procession of a Rose Queen elected from local girls, folk music performances, rose-distilling demonstrations, and the sale of rose water, rose oil, and rose-scented products. The valley during rose blooming season is one of Morocco's most beautiful sights — thousands of pink roses covering terraced fields against the backdrop of the High Atlas.

music_and_pop_culture.overview: Morocco's musical heritage is one of the richest and most diverse in Africa — a confluence of Amazigh oral musical traditions stretching back millennia, Andalusian classical music brought by refugees from Spain, Arab-Islamic court music, Sufi spiritual music, and the African-rooted Gnawa trance tradition. Modern Moroccan music blends all these with global influences — producing distinctive genres from chaabi street pop to the globally celebrated Gnawa fusion. Morocco gave the world Gnawa music, preserved Andalusian classical music when it was lost in Spain, and today produces internationally recognized artists from multiple genres.

traditional_music.name: Andalusian Classical Music (الموسيقى الأندلسية)
traditional_music.description: One of the world's great classical music traditions — and Morocco's most prestigious cultural inheritance. Andalusian music (also called Moorish music or Gharnati / Ala / Malhun depending on style) was developed in the Muslim courts of Al-Andalus (medieval Islamic Spain — particularly Cordoba, Seville, and Granada) from the 9th century onward by the legendary musician Ziryab (Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Nafi) and subsequent composers. When the Muslims and Jews were expelled from Spain in 1492, they brought this music to Morocco — preserving it in the cities of Fez (Ala style), Tetouan and Chefchaouen (Gharnati style), and Rabat and Sale. Morocco is now the primary custodian of Andalusian classical music — the music of Al-Andalus survives in Morocco far better than anywhere else. The nouba (نوبة) is the basic compositional form — a suite of movements in a specific mode (tab') proceeding through distinct rhythmic sections from slow to fast.
traditional_music.cities: Each imperial city maintains its own Andalusian music school (madrasa): Fez (Ala style — considered the most refined), Rabat (Ala), Tetouan (Gharnati — named for Granada/Gharnata), Chefchaouen (Gharnati)

traditional_music.name: Gnawa Music (الموسيقى الكناوية)
traditional_music.description: As described extensively in the ethnic groups section — Gnawa music is Morocco's most globally celebrated traditional music form. The gimbri/sintir bass lute and krakeb iron castanets create a hypnotic, deeply African soundscape. Gnawa music operates on multiple levels simultaneously — as community music, healing ceremony, spiritual practice, and artistic form. The collaboration between Gnawa maalems and international jazz and rock musicians has produced some of the most remarkable world music recordings of the late 20th and early 21st centuries.

traditional_music.name: Ahidous and Ahwach (الأحيدوس / الأحواش)
traditional_music.description: Two of the great Amazigh collective musical performance traditions of the High and Middle Atlas. Ahidous (of the Middle Atlas and eastern Morocco) features large groups of men and women arranged in a circle or line, alternating call-and-response vocal lines in complex interlocking patterns, accompanied only by bendir (large frame drums) and hand-clapping. The vocal harmonies of ahidous are among the most complex in African traditional music. Ahwach (of the Souss and High Atlas) involves larger groups — sometimes hundreds of participants — in dramatic musical performances with bendir, flute (nira), and collective singing, featuring spectacular synchronized movements and competitive group dynamics.
traditional_music.inscription: Ahidous was inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity list in 2008

traditional_music.name: Malhun (الملحون)
traditional_music.description: Classical Moroccan sung poetry — a tradition of performing classical Arabic and Darija poems to musical accompaniment. The poems (qasida) address love, spiritual longing, praise of the Prophet, and social commentary. Malhun is performed by a lead singer (sheikh or cheikha) accompanied by a small ensemble of traditional instruments. The malhun tradition has its own canon of classic poems and was the music of artisan guilds in the imperial cities — particularly in Fez, Meknes, Sale, and Marrakech.
traditional_music.cheikha: Cheikhate — female malhun and chaabi singers — are among Morocco's most important musical figures. Often from marginalized backgrounds, these women performers (cheikha) sang at celebrations and were simultaneously celebrated for their artistry and stigmatized for their unconventional social position.

traditional_music.name: Tagnawit / Tissintes (Amazigh Song)
traditional_music.description: The Amazigh oral musical tradition includes an enormous variety of song forms across the three language regions. Tachelhit musical forms include timawayin (love songs), izlan (poetic songs exchanged between men and women), and the collective ahwach. Tarifit music of the Rif has its own distinctive melodies and instruments. The ney flute (nira — Amazigh bamboo flute) and bendir (large frame drum) are the central instruments of the Amazigh musical tradition.

traditional_music.name: Reggada (الرقادة)
traditional_music.description: A trance music tradition from the Rif mountains — performed by the Aissawa and related brotherhoods and also as folk entertainment at celebrations. Characterized by the tbel (large double-headed drum), ghaita (a high-pitched double-reed oboe), and increasingly fast music that induces trance states. Reggada performances are physically intense — musicians and dancers sometimes enter possessed states.
traditional_music.region: Rif Mountains — northern Morocco (eastern Rif — Nador, Berkane)

modern_music.name: Chaabi (الشعبي — Moroccan Pop/Folk)
modern_music.description: Morocco's most popular everyday music — a broad genre rooted in malhun and Amazigh musical traditions, adapted to modern urban tastes. Chaabi (literally 'popular/of the people') features electric guitar (notably the sintir-influenced bass lines), banjo, darbuka, and vocals in Darija and Amazigh languages. Played at weddings, celebrations, and cafés across Morocco. Considered the music of working-class Morocco — sometimes dismissed by the elite but embraced by millions.
modern_music.jilala: A specific chaabi subgenre connected to Sufi trance practice

modern_music.name: Moroccan Hip-Hop and Rap
modern_music.description: A vibrant and growing hip-hop scene — predominantly in Darija, with Amazigh (Tamazight) and French elements. Moroccan hip-hop addresses social inequality, political criticism, youth frustration, and urban identity. The scene has produced artists of genuine quality who have gained international recognition.
key_artists: Don Bigg (محمد مرجان — the godfather of Moroccan rap — pioneered Darija-language rap from 2001)
Lbenj (لبنج — Abdelali Ababou — one of Morocco's most respected lyricists)
modern_music.don_bigg: Don Bigg — real name Mohammed Marjane — is the undisputed founding figure of Moroccan hip-hop. His 2008 album 'Casanegra' (which also became a hit Moroccan film) defined a generation. He writes exclusively in Darija — a political choice that asserts the legitimacy of Moroccan vernacular as an artistic language.

modern_music.name: Moroccan Pop and R&B
modern_music.description: Contemporary Moroccan pop blends Arabic, Amazigh, French, and Western influences in commercially polished productions.
key_artists: Saad Lamjarred (سعد لمجرد — the most commercially successful Moroccan pop artist — enormous audience across the Arab world)
Maher Zain (Lebanese-Swedish but massive in Morocco — Islamic pop)
Hindi Zahra (born in Khouribga — internationally acclaimed for her jazz-folk-Amazigh fusion)
Douzi (دوزي — Douzi Bilal — chaabi and pop fusion)

modern_music.name: Moroccan Metal and Rock
modern_music.description: A surprisingly active metal and rock underground exists in Morocco — particularly in Casablanca and Rabat. In 2003, the 'Satan's trial' saw 14 Moroccan metal fans arrested for allegedly promoting Satanism — a case that shocked the international music community and became a cause célèbre for freedom of expression. The case was eventually largely resolved, and Morocco's metal scene has continued, albeit cautiously.
modern_music.notable_band: Blaze Out — one of Morocco's best-known metal bands

legendary_artists.name: Jil Jilala (جيل جيلالة)
legendary_artists.description: One of Morocco's most revered musical groups — formed in 1972, they brought malhun, ahidous, and chaabi traditions together into an artistically serious body of work. Their music addressed spiritual, social, and cultural themes. Considered the greatest Moroccan musical group of the 20th century by many music scholars.

legendary_artists.name: Nass El Ghiwane (ناس الغيوان — People of the Ghiwane)
legendary_artists.description: Morocco's most politically significant musical group — often called 'the Moroccan Rolling Stones' (though this comparison doesn't capture their uniqueness). Formed in Casablanca's Hay Mohammadi working-class neighborhood in 1970, Nass El Ghiwane transformed Moroccan traditional music (gnawa, Sufi dhikr, malhun, chaabi) into politically charged, poetic rock music. Their instruments — sintir, hand drums, banjo — were unconventional. Their lyrics, in metaphor-rich Darija poetry, addressed poverty, injustice, exile, and freedom. They were beloved by the working class and feared by the authorities. Their influence on Moroccan music is incalculable.
members: Larbi Batma (died 1997 — the artistic soul of the group)

legendary_artists.name: Oum (Oum El Ghait Benali — أم الغيث بنعلي)
legendary_artists.description: Morocco's most internationally acclaimed living female artist — a jazz and soul singer who incorporates Gnawa, hassaniya, and Amazigh musical influences into a deeply personal artistic language. Her 2014 album 'Soul of Morocco' was critically acclaimed worldwide. She represents the synthesis of Morocco's musical heritage with global contemporary sensibility.
legendary_artists.international: Oum has performed at major international festivals and her music has introduced Moroccan musical culture to audiences in Europe, the Americas, and beyond

legendary_artists.name: Hindi Zahra
legendary_artists.description: Born in Khouribga (Morocco's phosphate capital) to an Amazigh family, Hindi Zahra moved to France and developed a unique musical world blending Moroccan Amazigh folk songs, jazz, blues, and English-language soul. Her debut album 'Handmade' (2010) was a major international success — recorded with one of the most minimal, organic production styles in contemporary world music. Her voice — raw, aching, and completely individual — is one of the most distinctive in African music.

cinema.description: Morocco has a significant and growing national film industry. Moroccan films address the country's social complexities — migration, gender, class, religious identity, and modernity — with increasing sophistication. Morocco is also one of the world's most used film locations — its diverse landscapes (desert, medina, mountains, coast) have hosted major international productions for decades.

moroccan_films.title: Casanegra (2008)
moroccan_films.description: The defining film of the 2000s Moroccan generation — a gritty coming-of-age story of two friends from Casablanca's working-class Hay Mohammadi neighborhood navigating drugs, crime, and dreams. The film's realistic Darija dialogue, its soundtrack (featuring Don Bigg), and its unflinching portrayal of urban poverty made it a cultural landmark.

moroccan_films.title: Miral (2010)
moroccan_films.description: International co-production directed by Julian Schnabel, set partly in Morocco, addressing Palestinian identity.

moroccan_films.title: Much Loved / Zin li fik (2015)
moroccan_films.description: Nabil Ayouch's controversial film about sex workers in Marrakech — banned in Morocco for its explicit portrayal of prostitution, it was selected as Morocco's entry for the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film. The controversy it generated opened a national conversation about censorship, social reality, and artistic freedom.

moroccan_films.title: Adam (2019)
moroccan_films.description: Maryam Touzani's first feature film — an intimate portrait of two women (a widowed baker and a young unmarried pregnant woman) in the Casablanca medina. Won critical acclaim across international film festivals.

cinema.international_productions: Morocco has hosted major international film productions for decades. Ouarzazate — in the pre-Saharan southeast — is the 'Hollywood of Africa': the Atlas Studios there are among the largest in the world and have hosted 'Lawrence of Arabia,' 'Gladiator,' 'The Mummy,' 'Babel,' 'Game of Thrones,' 'Kingdom of Heaven,' 'Ridley Scott's' multiple films, and many more. The city itself is a major film tourism destination.
cinema.atlas_studios: Atlas Studios in Ouarzazate is one of the world's largest film studios by outdoor set area — its permanent sets include ancient Egyptian streets, Roman armies, Tibetan monasteries, and medieval Arabic cities.

football.national_team: Atlas Lions (الأسود الأطلس — Lions of the Atlas Mountains)
football.2022_world_cup: Morocco's 2022 FIFA World Cup performance was one of the greatest stories in football history. The Atlas Lions became the first African and first Arab team to reach the semi-finals of a FIFA World Cup — defeating Spain (on penalties), Portugal (2–1, with an extraordinary team performance), and Belgium (2–0) before losing to France. The celebrations across Morocco, across Africa, and across the Arab world were extraordinary — millions took to the streets. The team's victories were seen as a victory for African football, for the Arab world, and for all those who had been told their time had not yet come.
key_players: Yassine Bounou (Bono — goalkeeper, one of the best in the world)
football.walid_regragui: Walid Regragui — the coach who led Morocco to the 2022 semi-finals. A former Moroccan international, he was appointed just three months before the World Cup and produced the greatest performance in African football history.

sports.athletics: Morocco has a proud tradition in long-distance running — Hicham El Guerrouj is the world record holder in the 1,500m and one mile, and multiple Olympic gold medalist. Said Aouita was a 5,000m world record holder in the 1980s. Morocco consistently produces world-class middle-distance runners.
sports.hicham_el_guerrouj: Hicham El Guerrouj — born in Berkane (northeastern Morocco) — is the greatest middle-distance runner in history. He holds world records in the 1,500m (3:26.00, set 1998) and the mile (3:43.13, set 1999) that have stood for over 25 years. He won Olympic gold at both the 1,500m and 5,000m at the 2004 Athens Olympics — the first athlete to win both events at the same Olympics since Paavo Nurmi in 1924.

administrative_divisions.system: Morocco is divided into 12 regions (جهات — jihat), each headed by a Wali (governor) appointed by the king. Each region is further divided into provinces and prefectures, then communes. The 2011 Constitution and subsequent regionalization reforms gave regions greater autonomy and elected regional councils.

regions.name: Tanger-Tetouan-Al Hoceima (طنجة-تطوان-الحسيمة)
regions.significance: Morocco's gateway to Europe — Tangier faces Spain across the Strait of Gibraltar (14km). The region includes the Rif Mountains (Tarifit Amazigh heartland), the Spanish enclaves of Ceuta and Melilla, and Chefchaouen (the Blue City).
known_for: Tangier (historic international zone, literary haunt of Matisse, Bowles, Burroughs, Ginsberg)
Chefchaouen (Blue City of the Rif)
Tetouan medina (UNESCO — finest Andalusian medina in Morocco)
Al Hoceima national park (Mediterranean coast)
Rif mountains and Amazigh culture

regions.name: L'Oriental (الشرق)
regions.significance: Morocco's eastern region — bordering Algeria. Home to the eastern Rif communities, the Moulouya River plain, and significant Tarifit and Tamazight speaking populations.
known_for: Beni Snassen Mountains (largest argan forest in eastern Morocco)
Figuig Oasis (Morocco's easternmost major oasis)
Nador lagoon (Mar Chica)

regions.name: Fès-Meknès (فاس-مكناس)
regions.significance: The historical and spiritual heart of Morocco — the twin imperial cities of Fez and Meknes. The most culturally significant region for Islamic scholarship, traditional craft, Andalusian heritage, and Moroccan identity.
known_for: Fes el-Bali (UNESCO — world's largest car-free urban area and most complete medieval Islamic city in existence)
Al-Qarawiyyin University (859 CE — the world's oldest continuously operating university)
Chouara Tannery (11th-century leather tannery still operating by traditional methods)
Meknes — Alaoui imperial city, Moulay Ismail's grandiose capital
Volubilis (UNESCO — best-preserved Roman city in Africa)
Moulay Idriss Zerhoun (Morocco's holiest city)
Ifrane (Morocco's 'Switzerland' — a mountain town with remarkable European-style architecture)
Middle Atlas cedar forests (Azrou — home to Barbary macaques)

regions.name: Rabat-Salé-Kénitra (الرباط-سلا-القنيطرة)
regions.significance: Morocco's capital region — administrative center, royal palaces, diplomatic quarter. Rabat is a UNESCO World Heritage city (the Hassan Tower, the Oudayas Kasbah, the Chellah). Salé, across the Bouregreg River, has its own historic medina and is Morocco's second-oldest city.
known_for: Rabat medina and royal palaces (UNESCO)
Hassan Tower (unfinished Almohad minaret — 12th century)
Oudayas Kasbah and garden (Almohad period)
Chellah necropolis (Merinid-era ruins on Roman Sala Colonia site)
Mohammed VI Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art

regions.name: Béni Mellal-Khénifra (بني ملال-خنيفرة)
regions.significance: The heart of the Middle Atlas — Tamazight Amazigh country. Home to Khénifra (capital of the Amazigh Zayan confederation, which mounted fierce resistance to French colonization under Mouha ou Hammou Zayani) and the spectacular Ouzoud Falls.
known_for: Ouzoud Falls (the highest waterfalls in the Maghreb — 110m)
Aït Benhaddou visits from the north
Tamazight Amazigh culture and carpet weaving
Bin el-Ouidane reservoir and lake

regions.name: Casablanca-Settat (الدار البيضاء-سطات)
regions.significance: Morocco's economic capital and most populous region. Casablanca (Casa / Dar el-Beida) is Morocco's financial, commercial, and industrial engine — the largest city in the Maghreb and one of Africa's most important financial centers.
known_for: Hassan II Mosque (the world's largest mosque in Africa and one of the largest in the world — built on the Atlantic Ocean, completed 1993, minaret 210m)
Casablanca's Art Deco architecture (1920s–1940s — one of the world's finest Art Deco urban environments)
Morocco Mall (one of Africa's largest shopping malls)
Quartier des Habous (a 1930s French-built 'new medina')
El Jadida (Portuguese Cistern — a stunning underground vaulted chamber, UNESCO World Heritage Site)
Mazagan (El Jadida's Portuguese fortified city — UNESCO)

regions.name: Marrakech-Safi (مراكش-آسفي)
regions.significance: The cultural and tourism heart of Morocco — Marrakech is the Red City and Morocco's most visited destination. Essaouira is the Atlantic jewel and Gnawa capital. Safi is Morocco's sardine capital and ceramics center.
known_for: Marrakech (Jemaa el-Fna — UNESCO Oral Heritage, Koutoubia Mosque, the souks, Bahia Palace, Saadian Tombs, Jardin Majorelle, Palais Badia)
Jemaa el-Fna square (the world's greatest outdoor theater — storytellers, musicians, acrobats, food vendors, henna artists, snake charmers, from dawn to midnight)
Essaouira (UNESCO blue and white Atlantic medina — Gnawa, wind, surfing, Jewish heritage)
El-Kelaa M'Gouna (Dadès Valley roses — Rose Festival)
Safi (ceramics — the great potters of Morocco)

regions.name: Drâa-Tafilalet (درعة-تافيلالت)
regions.significance: Morocco's pre-Saharan and Saharan region — gateway to the Sahara Desert, home to the Draa and Ziz river valleys, the Atlas Studios of Ouarzazate, the Erg Chebbi dunes (Merzouga), and the Tafilalt oasis (birthplace of the Alaoui dynasty).
known_for: Erg Chebbi (Merzouga) — Morocco's iconic golden sand dunes of the Sahara
Aït Benhaddou (UNESCO — the most spectacular mud-brick ksar in Morocco, set of Game of Thrones, Gladiator, Lawrence of Arabia)
Ouarzazate — 'Hollywood of Africa' and Atlas Studios
Todgha Gorge (dramatic limestone canyon — world-class rock climbing)
Dadès Gorge (Road of a Thousand Kasbahs)
Draa Valley (Morocco's longest valley — 200km of palms, kasbahs, and ancient ksour)

regions.name: Souss-Massa (سوس-ماسة)
regions.significance: The Amazigh heartland of the south — Tachelhit (Shilha) country. Agadir is Morocco's premier beach resort. The region produces Morocco's finest argan oil, almonds, and saffron. Taroudant is the 'grandmother of Marrakech.'
known_for: Agadir (Morocco's premier beach resort, rebuilt after 1960 earthquake)
Argan oil (the Souss Valley and Anti-Atlas are the world's only source of culinary argan oil)
Tafraoute (Anti-Atlas pink granite town surrounded by almond trees and painted rocks)
Taroudant (intact walled Amazigh city — the 'mini-Marrakech')
Tiznit (silver jewelry capital — Amazigh silver craftsmanship)
Souss-Massa National Park (flamingos, bald ibis, Atlantic coast wildlife)
Aït Baha and the Ammeln Valley (spectacular Anti-Atlas scenery)

regions.name: Guelmim-Oued Noun (كلميم-واد نون)
regions.significance: The gateway to the Sahara — Guelmim ('the blue city of the South') hosts the famous blue men camel market. The region is the transition between Morocco proper and the Western Sahara.
known_for: Guelmim camel market (Saturday — one of Morocco's most authentic markets)
Sidi Ifni (Art Deco Spanish colonial town on the Atlantic)
Plage Blanche (pristine, remote Atlantic beach)
Blue men tradition (Tuareg-influenced men wearing indigo-dyed cloth)

regions.name: Laâyoune-Sakia El Hamra (العيون-الساقية الحمراء)
regions.significance: The administrative capital of the disputed Western Sahara territory — administered by Morocco. Rich in phosphate deposits.
regions.status: Disputed territory — Morocco administers this as its Southern Provinces; the Polisario Front claims it as part of the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic; status unresolved at international level.

regions.name: Dakhla-Oued Ed Dahab (الداخلة-وادي الذهب)
regions.significance: The southernmost Moroccan-administered region — the Dakhla Peninsula on the Atlantic coast has become one of the world's premier kitesurfing destinations. Rich fishing grounds and phosphate reserves.
known_for: Dakhla (world-class kitesurfing — consistent Atlantic trade winds)
Rich Atlantic fishing grounds
Flamingo lagoon

proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: Patience is the key to relief.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Endurance through difficulty unlocks better days. One of the most used proverbs in Morocco — a people shaped by centuries of resilience against invasions, colonialism, and hardship.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: البعيد عن العين، بعيد عن القلب.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: Far from the eye, far from the heart.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Out of sight, out of mind — presence and proximity are the foundation of relationships. Deeply felt in Morocco, where diaspora emigration (to France, Spain, Belgium, Netherlands) separates millions of families.
Wisdom and intelligence are the finest jewelry a person can wear. More beautiful than gold or fine cloth — a mind well-used is the greatest beauty.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: الدار دار أبوك، ما تقول لها دوك.
proverbs_and_wisdom.transliteration: Ad-dar dar bouk, ma tgul liha duk.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: The house is your father's house — don't say 'move over' to it.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Respect the home and heritage you were given. Do not disrespect what your parents built for you — your foundation is precious.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: مع الذيب رح تعوي، ومع النحلة رح تجني العسل.
proverbs_and_wisdom.transliteration: M'a ddhib roh t'awwi, m'a n-nhla roh tjni l-'assl.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: With the wolf, you will howl; with the bee, you will harvest honey.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: You become your companions. Choose your friends wisely — their nature shapes yours. The company you keep determines the life you lead.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: آش قلت للناس يجي لك من عند الناس.
proverbs_and_wisdom.transliteration: Ash qult li-nnas yji lik m'and n-nnas.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: What you say about people comes back to you from people.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Your words return to you — speak of others with care and respect. Gossip and slander circle back to wound the speaker.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: إذا كان صاحبك عسل، ما تلحسوش كلو.
proverbs_and_wisdom.transliteration: Ila kan sahbak 'assl, ma tlhsush kullu.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: Even if your friend is honey, don't lick him all up.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Even the best relationships need boundaries. Do not exhaust or exploit your dearest friendships — moderation in all things, even love.
Let go of the past. Grieving over what cannot be changed wastes the present. Forgive, release, and move forward — one of Morocco's most concise and practical wisdoms.

proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: Hug the wall, and may God protect you.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Be cautious and humble — walk close to the wall (away from the center of danger). Humility and discretion are protection. Don't make yourself a target.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: راسك ما يوجعك إلا إذا حملت راس غيرك.
proverbs_and_wisdom.transliteration: Rassek ma iwja'k illa ila hmlt ras ghirak.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: Your head won't ache unless you carry another person's head.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Don't take on other people's problems as your own — unnecessary involvement in others' affairs creates your own suffering. Mind your own concerns first.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: ثلاثة أشياء لا يصلح بدونها البيت: الماء، النار، والمرأة الصالحة.
proverbs_and_wisdom.transliteration: Thlatha ashya' la yasluh biduniha l-bayt: al-ma', an-nar, wal-mar'a as-saliha.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: Three things without which no home is right: water, fire, and a righteous woman.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: The home is built on essentials — nourishment, warmth, and good character. A household is defined by the quality of those within it.
You do not need wealth to give — a kind, warm, or encouraging word is itself an act of generosity. Speech is power; use it to lift others.

travel_and_places.note: Morocco is Africa's most visited country and one of the world's great travel destinations — combining 5,000 years of civilization, some of the planet's finest medieval cities, Sahara Desert grandeur, Atlantic and Mediterranean coastlines, snow-capped Atlas mountains, extraordinary food and hospitality, and the overwhelming sensory richness of its medinas.

must_visit.name: Fez (Fès) — Fes el-Bali
must_visit.type: UNESCO World Heritage Site / Historical / Cultural
must_visit.description: The world's largest car-free urban area and Morocco's (and possibly the world's) most completely preserved medieval city — Fes el-Bali is a living, breathing 9th-century city of over 300,000 people navigating 9,000 streets, lanes, and culs-de-sac that have changed little in a thousand years. Founded in 789 CE by Moulay Idriss I and expanded by his son Idris II, Fez became Morocco's spiritual and intellectual capital — home to Al-Qarawiyyin University (the world's oldest continuously operating university, founded 859 CE by Fatima al-Fihri — a woman), hundreds of mosques and madrasas, the finest craftsmen in the Maghreb, and a maze of souks organized by craft (tanneries, leather, copper, weaving, spice, pottery). Getting lost in Fes el-Bali is not a problem — it is the experience.
highlights: Al-Qarawiyyin Mosque and University (859 CE)
Bou Inania Madrasa (finest Marinid architecture)
Al-Attarine Madrasa (near the Qarawiyyin)
Chouara Tannery (leather dyeing — 11th century, still operating)
Moulay Idriss II Zaouia (shrine of Fez's founding saint)
Mellah (Jewish quarter — fascinating synagogues and architecture)
Jnan Sbil Gardens
must_visit.insider_tip: Hire a certified guide for your first day in Fes el-Bali — the medina's labyrinthine complexity is genuinely disorienting and a good guide unlocks hidden masterpieces. Come back alone the second day when you have your bearings

must_visit.name: Marrakech — The Red City
must_visit.description: Morocco's most visited city and one of the world's great urban experiences. Marrakech — built in warm pink-red sandstone (giving it the 'Red City' name) — is simultaneously an ancient imperial city and a modern luxury destination. The heart of everything is Jemaa el-Fna (جامع الفناء) — the most extraordinary public square in Africa, declared UNESCO Oral Heritage of Humanity. By day: orange juice vendors, henna artists, snake charmers, and acrobats. By night: a city of 200+ food stalls billowing smoke, storytellers (hlayqiya) holding crowds in the oral tradition, musicians, healers, and tooth-pullers. The adjacent souks (markets organized by craft — leather, silver, spice, lamp, weaving, carpet) are the most atmospheric in Morocco.
highlights: Jemaa el-Fna square (all day and all night)
Koutoubia Mosque (12th-century Almohad minaret — the most perfect in the Maghreb)
Bahia Palace (19th century — most elaborately decorated Moroccan palace)
Saadian Tombs (16th century — sealed for 400 years, rediscovered 1917)
Jardin Majorelle (Yves Saint Laurent's magical blue garden)
Musée Yves Saint Laurent Marrakech
Medersa Ben Youssef (largest historic Quranic school in Morocco)
Mellah and Lazama Synagogue
The souks (copper, leather, spice, lamp, textile)
must_visit.insider_tip: Stay in a riad (traditional Moroccan courtyard house converted to accommodation) — the experience of a central fountain courtyard, rooftop terrace, and the sound of the medina at night is incomparable. Avoid tourist restaurants in Jemaa el-Fna — eat at the food stalls for an authentic experience at a fraction of the price

must_visit.name: Chefchaouen (الشاون) — The Blue City
must_visit.description: Morocco's most photographed city — and for good reason. Nestled in a valley of the Rif Mountains at 600m altitude, Chefchaouen's medina is painted in an extraordinary array of blues — from pale sky blue to deep midnight indigo — creating the most uniquely beautiful townscape in North Africa. Founded in 1471 CE as a small fortress (the name means 'look at the horns' — referring to the two mountain peaks above the town), Chefchaouen became a refuge for Andalusian Muslim and Jewish refugees fleeing the Reconquista. The blue tradition is said to have been introduced by Jewish refugees in the 1930s (blue symbolizing the sky and heaven in Jewish tradition) — though others attribute it to a later development. Whatever its origin, the blue medina of Chefchaouen is one of the world's most visually distinctive human settlements.
what_to_do: Simply wander the blue alleys at different times of day (light quality changes the blues dramatically)
Plaza Uta el-Hammam (main square — cafés, the Grand Mosque and kasbah)
Ras el-Maa waterfall and laundry wash (where local women wash clothes in the mountain stream)
Hike to the Spanish mosque (30 minutes above town — panoramic view of the blue city)
must_visit.insider_tip: Go in the early morning (7–9 AM) or late afternoon (4–6 PM) for the best light and fewest crowds. The blue streets are nearly photographic at these golden hours

must_visit.name: Sahara Desert — Erg Chebbi (Merzouga)
must_visit.description: Morocco's most iconic natural landscape — the great golden sand dunes of the Sahara. Erg Chebbi, near the village of Merzouga in southeastern Morocco, is the most spectacular dune field in Morocco — rising up to 150 meters, shifting in color from gold to deep orange to red as the sun moves. Camel trekking into the dunes at sunset, camping beneath a sky of extraordinary density of stars, and experiencing the Saharan silence — broken only by wind and the distant call to prayer — is one of the great travel experiences in Africa.
experience: Sunset camel trek into the dunes (2–3 hours)
Sahara overnight camp (traditional Berber tent or luxury camp)
Sunrise from a dune crest (worth the 5 AM wake-up)
must_visit.insider_tip: Merzouga village has a range of accommodation from basic guesthouses to luxury desert camps. The best camps are a 45-minute camel ride or 4WD drive into the dunes — away from the road-visible camps. Book in advance for December–February and March–April (peak season)

must_visit.name: Essaouira (الصويرة)
must_visit.description: One of Morocco's most beautiful and atmospheric cities — a white-and-blue UNESCO-listed Atlantic port medina where the wind always blows (Essaouira means 'the beautifully designed' in Amazigh). Built by the Sultan Mohammed ben Abdallah in the 18th century on the design of French architect Theodore Cornut, the city has a uniquely regular grid plan and colonnaded main streets unlike any other Moroccan medina. The ramparts overlook the thrashing Atlantic where seagulls and blue fishing boats compete for space. Essaouira has a uniquely multicultural heritage — Jewish, Gnawa, and Swahili trader communities have left their mark. The Gnawa & World Music Festival (June) transforms the city. Jimi Hendrix connection. World-class windsurfing and kitesurfing.
highlights: Sea ramparts (skala) overlooking the Atlantic
Fishing port at dawn
Gnawa music in the medina
Argan oil cooperatives nearby
Jewish mellah and synagogues
Crafts — thuya wood (unique to Essaouira, inlaid with lemon wood and mother of pearl)
Beach windsurfing/kitesurfing
must_visit.insider_tip: The fish grills by the port are one of Morocco's greatest budget food experiences — fresh fish chosen from the stall and grilled in front of you for very few dirhams

must_visit.name: Aït Benhaddou (آيت بنحدو)
must_visit.description: The most spectacular ksar (fortified village of mud-brick buildings) in Morocco and one of the most beautiful earthen architecture sites in the world. Aït Benhaddou has been continuously inhabited since the 11th century — its tiered mud towers, communal granaries (agadirs), and labyrinthine streets rise above the Ounila River with the snow-capped High Atlas behind. UNESCO World Heritage-listed since 1987. Film location: Lawrence of Arabia, Gladiator, Game of Thrones (Yunkai, Pentos), Kingdom of Heaven, The Mummy, The Jewel of the Nile, and many more.
must_visit.insider_tip: Cross the river on foot (summer) or by stepping stones (spring) to reach the ksar on the other side — the view from the top terrace at sunset, with the mountains turning pink, is extraordinary

must_visit.name: Volubilis (وليلي)
must_visit.description: The finest and best-preserved Roman archaeological site in Africa — Volubilis was the westernmost major city of the Roman Empire, capital of the province of Mauretania Tingitana. Founded as a Berber city and later Romanized, Volubilis reached its peak in the 2nd–3rd centuries CE with a population of 20,000. The site's extraordinary floor mosaics — some of the finest in the Roman world — are still in place. The Triumphal Arch of Caracalla, the Capitol, and the rows of tumbled columns and standing arches create one of Africa's most evocative ancient landscapes. Located between Meknes and Moulay Idriss Zerhoun.

must_visit.name: Toubkal National Park and Jbel Toubkal
must_visit.description: Jbel Toubkal (4,167m) is the highest peak in the Atlas Mountains, in North Africa, and in the Arab world — a demanding but accessible mountain trekking destination, typically completed in 2 days from the Amazigh village of Imlil (1,740m). The Toubkal National Park surrounding the peak offers extraordinary High Atlas trekking through Amazigh villages, walnut orchards, terraced fields, and dramatic rocky ridgelines. The park is also one of Morocco's most important Barbary macaque habitats.
must_visit.trekking: The standard Toubkal summit is approached via the Nelter refuge (3,207m — overnight stay) and summit push. Crampons required November–May. Guides available in Imlil.
must_visit.insider_tip: The valley of Ait Souka above Imlil, and the Tizi n'Ouanoums pass, offer spectacular High Atlas scenery without the summit crowds — consider a multi-day traverse rather than an out-and-back summit

must_visit.name: Tangier (طنجة)
must_visit.description: One of Africa's most fascinating cities — at the meeting point of the Atlantic and Mediterranean, Africa and Europe, East and West. Tangier has been an international city for most of its modern history — the Tangier International Zone (1923–1956) attracted artists, writers, spies, and adventurers from around the world: Paul Bowles (who lived in Tangier for 52 years and wrote 'The Sheltering Sky'), William S. Burroughs (who wrote 'Naked Lunch' in Tangier), Allen Ginsberg, Jack Kerouac, Matisse, Delacroix, and the Rolling Stones all had Tangier chapters. The Grand Socco, the medina, and the Kasbah quarter retain their international mystique. Under Mohammed VI's investment, Tangier has transformed into Morocco's fastest-developing city — with a new port (Tanger-Med, the largest in Africa), major industry, and a modern waterfront.
highlights: Kasbah museum (spectacular location with views of Spain)
Grand Socco and Petit Socco (main squares)
Paul Bowles's tomb (in the Christian cemetery)
Café de Paris and Café Hafa (legendary literary cafés)
Cap Malabata (view of Spain across the Strait)
must_visit.literary_note: Tangier's literary legacy — as a city of transgression, freedom, and the exotic — is one of the most distinctive in Africa

budget_tips: Morocco is very good value for international visitors — a full meal at a local restaurant (tajine, bread, water) costs 30–60 MAD (~$3–6)
Use public buses (CTM and Supratours are comfortable intercity options) rather than private taxis for long journeys — far cheaper and nearly as fast

The train network (ONCF) connects the major cities efficiently and comfortably — Casablanca to Marrakech in 3 hours, Casablanca to Fez in 4.5 hours, Casablanca to Tangier in 2.5 hours on the high-speed Al Boraq line
Staying in riads (traditional courtyard guesthouses) in medinas is often no more expensive than generic hotels — and the experience is incomparably better
The freshly squeezed orange juice sold from street carts across Morocco costs 4–8 MAD (~$0.40–0.80) — one of the world's great food bargains
Avoid tourist restaurants on Jemaa el-Fna's terrace level — descend to the food stalls in the square itself for a fraction of the price and a far more authentic experience
Many of Morocco's finest experiences are free or very cheap: wandering the medinas, watching Jemaa el-Fna, swimming at Atlantic beaches, hiking in the mountains
safety_tips: Morocco is one of Africa's safest countries for tourists — millions visit annually without incident. The main concerns are petty harassment and touts, particularly near tourist sites in Fez and Marrakech

In Fes el-Bali and Marrakech's medina: unofficial 'guides' who approach saying 'I am not a guide, I just want to help' are unofficial guides. Politely but firmly decline if you don't want guidance — say 'La shukran' (No, thank you)

Women traveling alone: Morocco is safe for solo female travelers but requires awareness. Modest dress (shoulders and knees covered in medinas and rural areas) significantly reduces unwanted attention. Sunglasses and confident walking body language help
Photographing people: always ask permission before photographing individuals — particularly in traditional contexts. Some will request payment
The Rif region (particularly around Ketama) is the heartland of Morocco's kif (cannabis) growing area — there is active pressure on visitors in this area to buy. Be firm and clear in declining

Road safety: Moroccan roads, particularly in the mountains and rural areas, can be challenging — aggressive drivers, narrow roads, and animals on road. If renting a car, drive defensively and do not drive at night in rural areas
Ramadan: during the holy month, be respectful of fasting — avoid eating, drinking, or smoking in public in traditional areas during daylight hours. Many tourist-oriented establishments remain open
Healthcare: major cities (Casablanca, Rabat, Marrakech) have good private hospitals. Travel insurance with medical evacuation coverage is essential for trekking and remote areas
